title: Nonlinear Compton scattering in a frequency-modulated field
abstract: When an electron is accelerated, it emits radiation. In the relativistic quantum realm the elementary radiation process is the emission of a single photon, a process known as nonlinear Compton scattering in the case of an electron moving in the presence of a strong electromagnetic wave. This process is typically described within the Furry picture, where the electromagnetic wave is described as a classical background field and the electron-positron field is quantized in the presence of that background field. Equivalently one can quantize the electron-positron field in the vacuum but then the photon emission process is described as a transition from an initial state to a final state both featuring, apart from the electron (the initial state) and the electron and the photon (the final state), the same coherent state of photons appropriately related to the electromagnetic wave. Here, we consider a more general situation where the initial and the final state feature the same squeezed coherent state. Then, we specialize to the case where the coherent state corresponds to a plane-wave field and the mostly populated modes of the coherent state are also squeezed. We show that, when quantum fluctuations induced by the squeezing in the coherent field are negligible, a condition well satisfied at available squeezing levels, the squeezing effects effectively reduce to a frequency modulation of the plane-wave field corresponding to the coherent state. By means of numerical examples we show that at already available squeezing levels the emission spectrum of nonlinear Compton scattering and the total photon yield can be altered significantly. Analytical explanations of the main numerical results are also provided.
summary: This paper explores how using squeezed light as a background laser field can control the radiation emitted during nonlinear Compton scattering. It demonstrates that the squeezing angle can be tuned to either enhance or suppress the photon yield and energy spectrum. This provides a new mechanism for controlling high-energy radiation processes using quantum optical techniques.
main_problem: Investigating how the squeezing of a background electromagnetic field can be used as a control parameter to alter the radiation spectrum and photon yield in nonlinear Compton scattering.
main_result: The authors show that squeezing in the background field effectively acts as a frequency modulation, which can significantly enhance or suppress the emitted photon energy spectrum and total photon yield depending on the squeezing angle.
method: The study uses the Furry picture of strong-field QED and a numerical kinetic approach to simulate the collision of ultrarelativistic electrons with a squeezed coherent plane-wave field.
model_or_system: An ultrarelativistic electron beam colliding with a strong, frequency-modulated electromagnetic plane wave modeled as a squeezed coherent state.
key_observables: Differential energy spectrum of emitted photons (dE/domega), total photon yield, and total emitted energy per electron.
important_parameters: Quantum nonlinearity parameter (chi_0), nonlinearity parameter (xi_0), squeezing amplitude (zeta_0), squeezing angle (theta_0), and Schwinger field (F_cr).
main_assumptions: Quantum fluctuations induced by squeezing are assumed to be negligible; the background field is treated as a classical external field (no depletion); and the electron dynamics are treated in a one-dimensional approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the differential energy spectra for different squeezing angles and the evolution of the nonlinearity parameter; Figure 2 compares the spectrum of a squeezed pulse to a non-squeezed pulse of equal total energy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear Compton scattering in squeezed fields, develops the theoretical framework using the Furry picture, demonstrates the equivalence of squeezing to frequency modulation, provides numerical simulations of electron-laser collisions, and concludes with an analysis of enhancement and suppression effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
